This is because of `AI_ADDRCONFIG`:

```
    If hints.ai_flags includes the AI_ADDRCONFIG flag, then IPv4 addresses are returned in the list pointed to by res only if the local system has at least one IPv4 address configured,  and  IPv6
    addresses are returned only if the local system has at least one IPv6 address configured.  The loopback address is not considered for this case as valid as a configured address.  This flag is
     useful on, for example, IPv4-only systems, to ensure that getaddrinfo() does not return IPv6 socket addresses that would always fail in connect(2) or bind(2).
```

and since there is no internet connectivity, no IP address is configured on any other interface except loopback. Perhaps it should be changed so that if `AI_ADDRCONFIG` fails then either

- the lookup is tried with `AI_NUMERICHOST` without `AI_ADDRCONFIG`,
- or the lookup itself is tried without `AI_ADDRCONFIG`, possibly with issuance of a warning or not...

If "blank state" means "reset all flag values to the default values", you can call `absl.flags.FLAGS.unparse_flags()`. This returns `FLAGS` to the state before flags are parsed from command line.

If "blank state" means "remove all flags as if no flags are defined", you can do something like:

```python
from absl import flags

for name in list(flags.FLAGS):
  delattr(flags.FLAGS, name)
```

But this means if you call other libraries that uses the flags they have defined previously, they won't work anymore (unless you define the same flags again). So I'm curious, what is your use case? I assume you already have some code that does this before Tensorflow switch to this library, do you have an example?

And if you just want a separate `FLAGS` instance, you can create a new one:

```python
from absl import flags

MY_FLAGS = flags.FlagValues()  # This is a separate "blank" instance different from the "global" flags.FLAGS instance.
```

Below is a simple gluon model I used (mainly copied from https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/gluon/gluon.html, only added wait_to_wait() call at the end in order to make sure OPs are executed):

```
# import dependencies
from __future__ import print_function
import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx
import mxnet.ndarray as F
import mxnet.gluon as gluon
from mxnet.gluon import nn
from mxnet import autograd

class Net(gluon.Block):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(Net, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        with self.name_scope():
            # layers created in name_scope will inherit name space
            # from parent layer.
            self.conv1 = nn.Conv2D(6, kernel_size=5)
            self.pool1 = nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=(2,2))
            self.conv2 = nn.Conv2D(16, kernel_size=5)
            self.pool2 = nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=(2,2))
            self.fc1 = nn.Dense(120)
            self.fc2 = nn.Dense(84)
            self.fc3 = nn.Dense(10)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool1(F.relu(self.conv1(x)))
        x = self.pool2(F.relu(self.conv2(x)))
        # 0 means copy over size from corresponding dimension.
        # -1 means infer size from the rest of dimensions.
        x = x.reshape((0, -1))
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = F.relu(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x

net = Net()
# Initialize on CPU. Replace with `mx.gpu(0)`, or `[mx.gpu(0), mx.gpu(1)]`,
# etc to use one or more GPUs.
net.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Xavier(), ctx=mx.cpu())

data = mx.nd.random_normal(shape=(10, 1, 32, 32))  # dummy data
output = net(data)
output.wait_to_read()
```

@sgillies Ah, I see. If it's acceptable to add new arbitrary keys to the top level geometry object, couldn't one just add `properties`? Or does that violate the spec somehow? I haven't read through it in detail yet--just skimming so far.

My point is: `geomet` converts between GeoJSON, WKB, and WKT, and there is no representation for FeatureCollections (that I could find) in WKT or WKB. So again, what kind of WKB/WKT output are you expecting from this GeoJSON? Your request is not clear. Please read https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Well-known_text for more details about WKT and WKB.

So you can't convert a FeatureCollection straight to WKB/WKT. What you _can_ do is extract the geometries from each feature in the the FeatureCollection's list and convert those individually--but that's a **one way** conversion:

```python
>>> from geomet import wkt
>>> fc = {'features': [{'geometry': {'coordinates': [125.6, 10.1], 'type': 'Point'},
   'properties': {'FID': 19987.0},
   'type': 'Feature'}],
 'type': 'FeatureCollection'}
>>> wkt.dumps(fc['features'][0]['geometry'])
'POINT (125.5999999999999943 10.0999999999999996)'
```

You can't take the `POINT ( ... )` and convert it back to a Feature or Feature collection. There's simply no way to store this extra metadata in WKT/WKB.

On Tue, Mar 21, 2017 at 3:15 PM David Bieber <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> I don't think adding a flag is appropriate here. If Fire provides a choice
> (sys.exit vs return) then it should provide that choice to the client code
> calling Fire, not to the end user. The end user won't have context as to
> whether it makes sense for the program to keep running after the call to
> Fire or not.
>
> When Fire hits a usage error the options are:
>
>    1. Display the error and then exit w/ a status code.
>    2. Display the error and return the error (wrapped in some object) to
>    the client.
>    3. Display the error and raise the error to the client.
>
> The downside of 1 is that it provides the client no way of handling the
> error.
> The downside of 2 is that the default use case (just calling fire.Fire)
> doesn't get proper status codes.
> The downside of 3 is that the default use case (just calling fire.Fire)
> will show a Python trace in the case of a usage error, which isn't the user
> experience we want.
>
> I lean toward option 2 because it makes getting proper status codes a
> simple 1-liner for people who want it (Something like result =
> fire.Fire(); result.ExitIfError(); exact syntax tbd.) and also allows the
> client the flexibility to handle the result of fire (error or not) however
> they chose. Option 1, while it gets status codes right, provides no way for
> people to handle usage errors themselves.

In my testing for https://github.com/jquery/jquery/pull/3151 I've discovered browsers are getting more & more aggressive at delaying computing stuff for elements out of the visible screen area. This doesn't just apply to iframes, though these optimizations cause different side effects in different browsers.

Even if the Edge team changed that here we'd still have a similar problem in many other browsers. In https://github.com/jquery/jquery/pull/3151 I even had to make sure the tests that create their own iframes are doing it at the top of the QUnit test window as otherwise they might have lost focus as well.

I think there's just no way around that (long term) than changing the TestSwarm layout to make sure the iframe is always visible and perhaps even changing our QUnit setup so that iframes created by tests appear at the top, not at the bottom. Perhaps it may even require changes in QUnit itself (cc @leobalter).

Note that you are not purposefully casting it to `int32`. That is just the type that Numpy returns when you query the shape. In the function `_compute_array_shapes`, you return the following:

    return tuple(input_shape), tuple(output_shape)

The elements of `input_shape` and `output_shape` in my case are `int32`. To fix the problem, I do something like this:

    input_shape = tuple([int(num) for num in input_shape])
    output_shape = tuple([int(num) for num in output_shape])
    return input_shape, output_shape

I have to disagree that the situation is exceptional and indicates a problem - I have a valid use case wrapping a real-world C++ library where a method return value may sometimes be nullptr, but when it is not nullptr a keep_alive<0,1> is required. 

If it is acceptable for patient to be None, acting as a no-op with no error raised, then it makes sense to me that it would be acceptable for nurse to be None, also acting as a no-op with no error raised. Logically "Nurse, please keep nobody alive" and "Nobody, please keep patient alive" both result in no keep-alive.

With this patch, having a None nurse becomes equivalent to having a valid nurse that is immediately garbage collected, in that the end result  for both is a patient that is not kept alive by a nurse. To me, the following pseudo-code should have the same end-result, and I was surprised that they do not:

nurse = Nurse()
patient = Patient()
keep_alive(nurse, patient)
patient = None
nurse = None
gc.collect()

nurse = None
patient = Patient()
keep_alive(nurse, patient)
patient = None
gc.collect()

Well, I can't say I find segfaults in high-level C++ interfaces any more acceptable than in Python (and I'm not sure anyone will be using `None` as control flow in Python so that the exception must be `TypeError`), but if this saves people from writing lots of lambda wrappers then I guess I don't have any strong objection.

I've got frustrating feedback from others using SoS to analyze many small tasks. Also as my own analysis scale up (77K analysis unit for 40 phenotypes), it seems I have to remove `~/.sos/tasks` on daily basis. The cluster system is not happy with too many files in the home directory. The problem is also related to having to restart the submission a few times, and that seems to rapidly grow the number of tasks files accumulated.

Having global `~/.sos/tasks` is also problematic because when I remove `~/.sos/tasks` I lose it for other projects, too.

My questions are:

1. How many files does SoS write to `~/.sos/tasks` for per task, or per output?
2. Can we make sure re-starting the same script does not generate additional task files? That is, try to keep it strictly one file per output. That way, minor change to the script will not lead to  duplicated task files.
3. Can we have some option to use `.sos` not `~/.sos`? I understand our design goal, but in my context it brings more troubles than convenience.

I think as long as we can make it strictly per file per output (or per file-set per output) things will be more under control. I understand you might not have these large scale test applications, but I guess we can at least start checking the code and think about at which steps we can reduce number of files stored?

Shutdown will not poweroff any VMs before running. It is the equivalent of running "/sbin/poweroff" in the console/ssh.
The force option is just letting you run the command without being in maintenance mode first. Maintenance mode would be what relocates the VMs or requires you to power off first.

ESXi will not power on these VMs automatically when it starts back up by default. You would have to use the Autostart feature and enable it for the VMs you want to power on with the host.
From the vSphere Web Client, that would be under the host -> Configure -> VM Startup/Shutdown.

> The only unfortunate thing about this is that get_output_shape_for() returning a tuple and returning a list would mean different things.

I think this is okay, but it might be counter-intuitive for some who are less familiar with Python / numpy and who might assume that `(1, 2)` and `[1, 2]` are the same thing. TensorFlow sets a bad example here by using lists for shape specifications by default (I've seen this in their docs, at least).

> 3. MergeLayer and DictMergeLayer either accept a list/dictionary of layers, or a single layer, which is then expected to return multiple outputs.

What if you want to mix and match, i.e. have two of the inputs be provided by a single layer, and then a third by another layer? I guess this is an extremely uncommon use case so it might be okay to require the use of `SplitLayer`s in this case, if it simplifies the code considerably (which I think it might).

> 5. The Layer base class gets an __index__ method that returns such a SplitLayer stacked on top, so you can do layer = LSTMLayer(...); cell = layer['cell'] to access one of the layer outputs.

Neat :) A little bit magical, but very intuitive.

> I think I like (B) a little more, because in retrospect I think the ideal choice would have been that all layers' get_output_for/get_shape_for/etc. are required to accept and produce an OrderedDict, and __init__ is required to accept an OrderedDict of layers 

I understand that you're not proposing to actually make this change, but nevertheless: I think this goes against our design guidelines of "pragmatism" and "restraint". Most use cases do not require layers with multiple outputs (or multiple inputs, for that matter), and people who do not encounter such use cases should not be forced to complicate their code with `OrderedDict`s.

Re: `split()` as a helper function in proposal C2: could it not be a method on a layer with multiple outputs instead? That would be more natural imo. Especially because we would probably want `lasagne.layers.split()` as an alias for `SplitLayer` (we usually have 'functional' aliases for layers that are not typically considered to be actual neural network layers, such as `concat` and `dropout`).

Thanks a lot for the clear overview of different options. I haven't really decided yet which one I'd be in favour of. I like the idea of making multiple output layers indexable very much though. So far I think I like proposal A best, because it keeps things simple for 'regular' layers, which are the most common use case. We are already treating `MergeLayers` specially in `get_output()` anyway, so having two `if` branches in there instead of one is not a huge deal to me.

> On the other hand, I don't know how to implement the check for the Theano version. [...] Any suggestions on how to implement this?

Usually the best way is to either check for the specific functionality (using `hasattr`, on a module, for example), or just try to use the specific functionality and fall back if it doesn't. Looking at the diff of the PR that introduced variable pooling to Theano (https://github.com/Theano/Theano/pull/4756/files#diff-7e13e905a5451cdcfd78519fcca02c2fR235), a possible hook might be the `__props__` or the `__init__` keyword arguments of the `Pool` op. As long as `padding` is in there, it doesn't support variable window sizes and strides (we cannot use the window size or strides because they changed names afterwards, but we can rely on `padding` staying like this for now). So the test would be either:
```python
if 'padding' in theano.signal.pool.Pool.__props__:
    # not supported
else:
    # supported
```
or:
```python
try:
    pool_2d(T.tensor4(), ws=T.ivector(), stride=T.ivector())
except ValueError:
    # unsupported
else:
    # supported
```
I'd prefer the latter. It relies less on internals and is easier to understand.

By the way, back in 2012, batchwise dropout wasn't giving me as good results on MNIST as itemwise dropout (i.e., batchwise dropout was not on par with the 2012 dropout paper).

I think maybe we're talking about different things - I was trying to implement [this paper from feb 2015](http://arxiv.org/pdf/1502.02478v1.pdf). It use submatrices to avoid calculating dropped-out activations, and at least the way I was doing it required modifying the `W` matrices of layers following the `BatchwiseDropoutLayer`. Since that can't be done until the full network is constructed, I was using helper functions.

I think that part of this line blurring can be helped by combining `Query` objects and pipelines. If the `Query` object is handed to the pipeline in some way that it could understand then we could write down somewhere exactly how you asked for the multi-header results. At this point the data could be bundled together into one "header" which contains metadata from all the incoming headers and interleaves the events (maybe by event timestamp?). This single header would pass into the pipeline to be filtered as needed (max image intensity, sequence number mod 4?). By storing the provenance of the pipeline we can reproduce the results of this header + event level filter (we can get the headers from the query, combine them in the same way, and run them through the exact same pipeline).

@yungchin this looks great, I'm excited to get it into master. My main thought in response to your design considerations is that I'd prefer to avoid adding switchable modes to this system too eagerly. I think we should figure out which mode is the most likely to be generally useful and present it as the only option. If after that we find that users would like tweaks that support different use cases, we can keep these mode suggestions in mind. (This doesn't include enabling/disabling delivery reporting entirely - I agree that's necessary).

I think we should stick with reporting all deliveries, not just errors. The original impetus for this feature was a user who needed confirmation that each message had been produced, and I think it's fine to risk providing slightly more information than some use cases will require. Making this switchable seems like needless machinery for too small a benefit.

If your Django app doesn't know its own public hostname (because Django is behind a proxy that doesn't set X-Forwarded-Host, e.g.), you'll need to set [MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_URL](http://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/latest/esps/mandrill/#std:setting-ANYMAIL_MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_URL) to the exact webhook url you entered into Mandrill's dashboard.

Without that setting, Mandrill is computing a signature for something like `https://yoursite.example.com/anymail/mandrill/tracking/`, but Anymail thinks it was called at something like `http://127.0.0.1:8000/anymail/mandrill/tracking/` -- so the signatures don't match. 

(I'm making a note to update that error message to include the url Anymail thinks it was called at.)

If that's not the issue, it would be helpful to know things like Anymail and Django versions, whether the webhook has ever worked (and if so, what changed since then). 

You could also scan up the stack trace in Sentry to find where anymail.webhooks.mandrill...validate_request is raising the error -- Sentry may have captured more information there that would help your investigation. In particular, `signed_data` will start with the url Anymail thinks it was called at (followed by your webhook key and the request params that were signed).

> Got it, thanks for the explanation. Mind if I change the integration
tests to use `-v`?

I think we still want `-vv` on the client side. If you want to write up a PR to run `-vv` on the client side but `-v` for boulder, I'll merge it.

> Also, what is the reason for the `* 10` when keeping track of the
logging level?

The predefined [logging levels](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#logging-levels) are given the numeric values 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. By multiplying by 10, one count of `-v` causes you to jump to the next logging level.

> Why do we multiple things by negative?

The way the `count` [action](https://docs.python.org/2/library/argparse.html#action) works in argparse is we start at default value, adding 1 to the result every time the flag is seen. Since we want `-v` to result in a lower numeric logging level, we make the default -3, causing one instance of `-v` to result in a count of -2, which when multiplied by -1 and 10, we get 20 which is the correct logging level (see the logging level link above).

Steps to reproduce one example of not unescaping correctly:

1. On a system with Augeas 1.4+, create an HTTP vhost with a comma in the file name.
2. Use Certbot using the `run` subcommand (e.g. not `certonly`).
3. When prompted, select the vhost with a comma in the filename.
4. Get told "we only support configurations with one vhost per file".

Why does this happen? In [make_vhost_ssl](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/configurator.py#L750), avail_fp and ssl_vp are unescaped paths. We then pass this path back into Augeas, `match` returns an empty list, and since the length of the list isn't 1, we assume it's greater than 1, and output the "...vhost per file" error message.

I'm unfortunately strongly in favor of reading through the entire plugin, reasoning about where paths should and should not be escaped, and creating a system to ensure this happens.

> To get a SSL security certificate on my machine, I have to download unsigned and untrusted code from a public web site maintained by an unknown number of arbitrary contributors, and then run this code on my servers.

All code you have ever run by us has been signed. Perhaps our documentation wasn't very clear on how to check this signature, but regardless, the signature has always existed.

We have always uploaded signatures of our packages to PyPI. `certbot-auto`/`letsencrypt-auto` is also signed and the signatures are verified when downloading any updates. There is a signature generated and checked using `openssl` that `certbot-auto` uses internally. Additionally, there is a PGP signature which can be checked using our instructions [here](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/intro.html#installation).

> Upon first-run, I'm greeted like this:-

>[sudo] password for gitcnd:

Unfortunately, in some cases we do ask for your `sudo` password without explaining why/what we plan to do with it. While you can prove the script came from us, it's just bad from a usability perspective to ask for the users `sudo` password with no context. Despite the fact that `certbot-auto`/`letsencrypt-auto` is supposed to be a temporary workaround while OS packages are developed, this should be fixed.

> ... this code then reaches out without warning or permission to assorted internet servers...

> and who knows what happens next, because after many dozens of junk dependancies, something died and it all failed.

All in all, I think the main issue here is poor documentation and usability design. Perhaps our documentation on how to verify our signatures can be improved. The shallow dep list is a great idea. I have a couple minor concerns:

1. What happens when the oldest version of dep we need to support for Python 2.7 distro packaging doesn't work on a newer Python like 3.6?
2. What happens when a new version of a dependency drops support for a Python version we're still supporting?
3. Do we want to maintain a deep list of dependencies of the plugin that aren't maintained by us for increased stability for devs and real testing of old package versions? For example, if a package didn't pin back the next major versions of its dependencies, an old version of the package may break.

But I'm not planning on splitting everything immediately right now. To get a basic version of these tests up and running, I'm planning on sticking with our current test structure and have the tests for the oldest dependency versions ordered so the plugins that allow an older acme/certbot versions are tested before the ones that require a newer version. With the good idea of not looking backward to see what versions are supported, the only difference will be the last packaged version and plugins that require changes from master. If you have a problem with this approach please let me know.

Continuing on my separate repo tangent if you don't mind, the biggest concern I have is trying to make things sane for devs that are adding changes to acme/certbot to be immediately used in a plugin. For example, we often add functionality for the Apache/Nginx that we expect to be more generally useful to `certbot/plugins/common.py`. Will devs be able to (easily) check out a branch of acme/certbot to use for plugin development or do they have to wait for the changes like this to land in master before working on the plugin? How do we keep the version of acme/certbot used for plugin development up to date with master? Hopefully they don't have to recreate the virtual environment each time they want to pull in the most recent changes. I've personally never worked on a project structured in this way so if you have ideas here I'd love to hear them.

@joohoi Gentoo uses `OpenRC` by default, but `systemd` is also officially supported. Personally, I'm not familiar with `systemd`. But I'm sure it has something to do with the `apache2ctl gracefull` command, where `/usr/sbin/apache2ctl` is just a "forwarding script" towards `/etc/init.d/apache2`. Which, as far as I can tell, is indeed an `OpenRC` script, which are disabled when `systemd` is used.

So, I think there are two options: a) start supporting a non-supported init system (no clue how much work that will take) or b) check for the `systemd` init system and tell the user we're sorry, but are not supporting `systemd` currently.

I feel that this is a rather unusual configuration, at least in large scale, and managing it would require shelling out to, and expecting external tools to be available to handle within Certbot. Because of that, I believe supporting it withing Certbot itself would probably make little sense.

However, to mitigate the issue for @ivordurham <https://github.com/ivordurham>  usecase, I feel using renewal deployment hooks would be in order. There's some documentation <https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewing-certificates>  how to use the hooks, and different approaches of deploying one. This pretty much means creating a small shell script, with the required commands, and deploying it to /etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks/deploy. If you have multiple certificates on the same machine, of which not every requiring this functionality, you can check environment variable set by Certbot to verify that the certificate being deployed is the correct one. The env variables for this use are: RENEWED_DOMAINS and RENEWED_LINEAGE.

There was a split for the 5.x branch, and 5.x now trails master, while 4.x is managed separately.

You should be looking at the [4.x branch Changelog](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/4.x/docs/Changelog.rst), or at the [official documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/4.3/versions.html), which shows all of the documentation changes for minor releases since 3.3 by way of a drop-down.  

Again, I apologize for the inconvenience, but I got in the habit of tracking the most recent version I could trust until I updated tests for Travis.  Please understand the difficulties of spanning major versions by using an _older_ version of the python API (2.4.1).  I didn't want to have anything break.  I was nervous about future code-arounds biting users.  Instead, I had them unable to use new versions of ES until I had updated Curator.  However, once I had integration testing with _all_ current releases of all minor branches of Elasticsearch, I felt safe to put a blanket support for all 5.x versions.  Before that, Curator 4 supported ES 5 _insofar as you had the most recent version of Curator._  

This is why, with Curator 5, I opted to forgo any sort of reverse compatibility, so I wouldn't have to do that again.

See [4.0.0](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.0.0/curator/settings.py), [4.1.0](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.1.0/curator/defaults/settings.py#L7), [4.2.0](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.2.0/curator/defaults/settings.py#L7), [4.2.3](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.2.3/curator/defaults/settings.py#L6), [4.2.4](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.2.4/curator/defaults/settings.py#L6), [4.2.6](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.2.6/curator/defaults/settings.py#L6).

As stated, now I track all new versions in Travis: [4.2.6](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.2.6/.travis.yml#L9-L15), [4.3.1](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.3.1/.travis.yml#L10-L19), [5.0.2](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.0.2/.travis.yml#L10-L14).

I apologize again for the inconvenience, but there's nothing I will be changing, since the most recent version of Curator 4 works just fine with ES 5.x.

I agree that's the way that involves minimal changes, but I think we should think about it more carefully and make a larger change to produce a more coherent system.

Specifically, I don't think it should be based on installing files in the config system. The more files affect the config, the greater the risk of confusion from the file you're looking at being overridden elsewhere.

What I'd like to see instead is a system where nbextensions install into the nbextensions directory a separate json file with some metadata, including how to load the module. E.g.

```json
{
    "name": "cite2c",
    "description": "Insert citations from Zotero",
    "url": "https://github.com/takluyver/cite2c"
    "load": "cite2c/main"
}
```

Any extensions with these files would be loaded by default, and the user could use config to *disable* them. This has a couple of other benefits:

- It becomes easy to find out what extensions are installed on a system - and therefore to build an interface to conveniently switch them on and off.
- It reduces the likelihood of error messages from extensions which are removed but still configured as enabled.

Let's just fix shift-j/k. Here is one possible decision set we can use
inside `extend_marked`:

(old selection) -> (new selection) = (action in extend_marked)
marked -> unmarked = do nothing
marked -> marked = do nothing
unmarked -> unmarked = mark the old one
unmarked -> marked = unmark the new one

I am pretty sure gives the standard behavior of shift-j/k and even works
for multiple separately contiguous blocks selected this way.

@mrcslws 

After spending some time adjusting subtle differences in Java to get exactly the same output as with the Python version, I want to point out that the previous code had a slight functional bug that doesn't get exposed by the unit tests. 

At the end of ```Connections.computeActivity()``` the ```SegmentOverlap```(s) for both the matching and active [segments were being sorted by a "bad" key](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/connections.py#L526).

The key: ```(s.segment.cell * self.maxSegmentsPerCell
                            + s.segment.idx)```

really was arbitrary and the "s.segment.idx" was actually **always zero** and had **no** effect!

These sorted segments are used in ```t+1``` as the entries to the new ```groupby2()``` function at the beginning of TemporalMemory.compute() - and _totally_ determine the quality and efficiency of groupby2 groupings which then determines what column's segments get marked as "active" and which don't.

At first, I instinctually grouped by the ascending columns of the cells the segments belonged to and I assumed that the above key was doing the same. I was getting superior results but not equal results (my anomaly scores were better) - which was a weird unwanted result because I was shooting for absolute parity. Then I took another look at the key and examined what it was actually producing which led to my discovery of the bug.

**Summary:**
I no longer am a fan of the "groupby2()" technique (instead preferring your first "walking technique with the ```ExcitedColumnGenerator```s; because it **hides** a potential weirdness in the selection of the active and winner cells.

If we "go" with this "groupby2" technique, please be very careful to sort the source lists at the end of ```Connections.computeActivity()``` carefully and prove to yourself that you are getting the "groupings" that allow for the best TM results?

I think its slightly better since its at least an initialized list, rather than a class constant (which iirc yours was @zephyr?). I believe my suggestion was to use the single validate_config function to call all the specific validate functions directly, rather than having a variable hold them. It makes the builder slightly simpler, since that var is not used anywhere outside that function anyways, which I think could apply here as well @econchick.  

Unrelated to this specific PR, it seems like we have a lot of code for config validation. My opinion is that unless there's some specific thing we're checking (trailing '.', number in a certain range, etc.), we should just default to a KeyError thrown by the init of the plugin itself, logged or outputted in a way the user knows what class threw it, so they know which config to change (probably in the plugin loader, rather than a builder since it is repeated code across all builders). If we need a longer description of why a config parameter is needed, I think that should live in docs to keep the code simpler.

Since there's been some discussion about standardizing how we do config validation, but there's been no consensus that I know of, I don't think we're ready for consistency. I assumed that was something we were going to handle later with factoring out of all the plugins in one go.

Nope, the `Name` object is the attribute value. It's can be a little confusing. In this case, the `NameValue` contains the actual name string (e.g., "Peter"). However, the `Name` object contains both the value and the type of the name string; according to the KMIP specification, these name types include `Uninterpreted Text String` and `URI`. The value of a `Name` attribute object contains two things, the name itself ("Peter") and the name type ("Uninterpreted Text String").

In terms of the overall `Attribute` structure, it would look like this:

    Attribute:
        Attribute Name: Name
        Attribute Index (optional): 1
        Attribute Value:
            Name Value: Peter
            Name Type: Uninterpreted Text String

It's a complex question.
`__del__` is called on session destruction.
It could be even interpreter finalization stage if the session has global scope (we discourage this approach but still support it).

If the loop is closed before `ClientSession.__del__` -- nothing to do.

On other hand the session could be dropped in coroutine context:
```
async def f():
    session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
    ...
    del session  # or just implicit decref on frame destruction
```   
In this case the loop is running, `loop.run_until_complete()` is prohibited.

The check should analyze not only middlewares but nested applications also.

Hi @madduci 

This is a side issue of the Windows path length limitation. There is a flag in packages that defines an alternative behavior: http://docs.conan.io/en/latest/reference/conanfile.html#short-paths

The behavior of this path shortener is:
- Take the location of your local conan storage, typically C:/User/name/.conan
- Take the drive C:
- Use a shorter path C:/.conan/short_path/...

All the that are marked to be shortened in the local conan storage implements a "portable symlink" implemented with that .conan_link file. Yes, the behavior of symlinks in Win was problematic, so we finally workarounded it with a file.

You could remove the ``short_paths=True`` from recipes, but if you are using packages with long paths (Boost, Qt, and others), they will most likely fail in Windows due to that limitation.

Hi @geertn and @embolalia,

Thanks for the feedback and sorry for not replying here earlier.

The behavior described in this issue is indeed how pip-accel was originally designed to work, so not exactly a bug. When the (name, version) requirements specified on the command line can be satisfied based on cached source and binary distributions on the local file system pip-accel would install those and not go looking for updates on remote package indexes.

However just because pip-accel was designed like this doesn't mean that this behavior of ignoring the `-U`, `--upgrade` option hasn't annoyed the hell out of me :-). This is why I've just released pip-accel 0.34 which changes behavior in this respect:

- If `-U` or `--upgrade` is given then new versions in remote indexes will be discovered and preferred, regardless of whether older locally cached versions can be used to satisfy the requirements.

- If `-U` and `--upgrade` aren't given and the requirements can be satisfied from the local file system then new versions in remote indexes will not be discovered.

I believe that with this change things should work as intuitively as possible. Sorry about the confusion caused by the behavior of previous versions!

When the device disappears and reapers while the port is open, it won't work under the old name. Other operating systems tend to assign a new device in such cases, e.g. under Linux, it may become /dv/ttyUSB1 and /dev/ttyUSB0 will disappear as soon as the application closes the port. Windows tries to assign the same COM port for the device so the old instance shadows the new one, or something like that.

I know of no workaround. Maybe there are solutions, e.g. if the device is connected through a hub, the hub could be disabled or the device itself could be powered down. USB has such commands but i do not know of a Windows API that exposes these (never looked for that).
Alternatively, make your device slower so that the PC can close the port before the device reappears on USB... like an additional delay at startup (maybe only when the device is in test mode).

A silly alternative would be to use two (or more?) alternating serial numbers for the device, so it would get a different COM port after each reboot of the device. But i would not suggest to really do this.

You are probably confusing the include_**path** from Cython with the include_**dirs** from distutils. I know it's confusing, but the first is how Cython looks for `.pxi` and `.pxd` files and the second is how distutils looks for `.h` files when compiling the sources.

You need `sys.path` in the include_**path** which will then use Cython machinery to automatically add the correct include_**dirs**.

I do realize now that this only works properly if the `ext_modules` are generated by Cython. In other words, if `setup.py` looks like
```
setup(...,
    ext_modules=cythonize(...)
)
```
If this is not the case, you indeed need to manually add `include_dirs`. We could provide a function like the one you describe, although I would consider that "unsupported".

```python

def get_ids(Doc doc, eos='-EOS-', bos='-BOS-'):
    cdef np.ndarray ids_py = numpy.zeros((len(doc)+2,), dtype='uint64')
    ids = <uint64*>ids_py.data
    ids[0] = doc.vocab.strings[eos]
    cdef int i
    for i in range(doc.length):
        ids[i-1] = doc.c[i].lex.orth
    ids[doc.length] = doc.vocab.strings[bos]
    return ids_py
```

I think a `self._meta_data['version'] = #` is the correct data structure.
There is a nice side-effect of sending a request during `ManagementRoot` construction, it makes the `()` _always_ send a request, when used in the SDK.   Currently `ManagementRoot` construction is the sole exception to that rule.

I don't like exceptions.   Please implement a request in the construction of the `ManagementRoot` that obtains the `version` of the device and assigns `self._meta_data['version']` to its value.

I have been able to narrow down the problems to 2 root causes: 

Issue 1: Problems with 'tmos_version' property where it is not set on some unit tests. Due to us faking instantiation of ManagementRoot(BIGIP):

Resolution: 
````

    @property
    def tmos_version(self):
        # We return tmos_version, or if None,
        # we grab it and return value via _get_tmos_version(),
        # at the same time updating _meta_data['tmos_version']
        return self._meta_data['tmos_version'] or self._get_tmos_version()

````
In addition to the above you would need to go through the tests we have and add manually the following:
````
instance._meta_data['tmos_version']
````
Here instance will be whatever fake BIGIP or MR object we create.

Issue 2: Sometimes the link to get tmos version gets duplicated so instead of getting:
https://yourhost:443/mgmt/tm/sys/ we get https://yourhost:443/mgmt/tm/sys/tm/sys. 

This is due to the following:

````

class BigIP(ManagementRoot):
    """A shim class used to access the default config resources in 'mgmt/tm.'

    This class is solely implemented for backwards compatibility.
    """
    def __init__(self, hostname, username, password, **kwargs):
        super(BigIP, self).__init__(hostname, username, password, **kwargs)
        self._meta_data['uri'] = self._meta_data['uri'] + 'tm/'
        self._meta_data['allowed_lazy_attributes'] =\
            [TmAuth, TmCm, Ltm, Net, TmShared, Sys, Transactions]
````

Lots of our tests utilize BigIP(host, user, pass) syntax which explains why this is happening, as we append the /mgmt/ with tm/:

````
self._meta_data['uri'] = self._meta_data['uri'] + 'tm/'

````
our MR method: _get_tmos_version does this: 
````
base_uri = self._meta_data['uri'] + 'tm/sys/'
````
I will need to think on how to address, some uri processing to check for existence of tm/ maybe ? As adding a method into BIGIP just for that is not the right thing to do.

> You should probably take a look at how iter_messages is implemented

I have been following your suggestion, but I'm still not able to fetch joiner timestamps earlier than [last_id - 100](https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/blob/master/telethon/client/messages.py#L144).

After reading through [``iter_messages``](https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/blob/master/telethon/client/messages.py#L24), I think it's magic is to update ``offset_id`` in every call to gradually fetch all historical messages. 

However, 

- ``GetAdminLogRequest`` doesn't take ``offset_id`` as its parameter.

WebSocket support message fragmentation (splitting a single WebSocket message into smaller frames - but with no reordering at the frame nor message level).

AutobahnPython and Crossbar.io both support the automatic fragmentation of _outgoing_ WebSocket messages into frames.

Both also support setting a max on frame and message size of _incoming_ messages.

What is missing is setting a max frame and/or message size of _outgoing_ WebSocket messages.

This should be added in AB Py, and it should be added purely at the WebSocket level (not WAMP).

So the first thing we need to do is extend the `setProtocolOptions` with 2 new parameters:

* `maxOutgoingFramePayloadSize`
* `maxOutgoingMessagePayloadSize`

> Note: auto-fragmentation of outgoing message can be controlled using `autoFragmentSize` parameter in above.

We must take care with what we are doing here:

`SimpleItem`s have a `typelist` attribute which is used to check the incoming value against to make sure it matches. `ComponentItem`s do not have a `typelist` attribute and instead use the `category` to make sure that the value being passed in implements the correct plugin.

Both schema item types have a `defvalue` but they works very differently in each case. With a `SimpleItem` it works as you would expect where when the `GangaObject` instance is created, that schema value is set to a copy of the thing in `defvalue`. With `ComponentItem`s the `defvalue` can be either `None` (the default) in which case that schema value will be a new instance of the `category`'s default plugin or it is set to a string (i.e. `'Dirac'`) in which case the object instance will have its schema value set to a new instance of that type (found by looking in the plugin system, in the correct `category` and then for a plugin which matches `Dirac`).

Using `typelist` on a `ComponentItem` should never make sense and is probably ignored. Likewise, using `defvalue` on a will usually not do what you want. All you can do with a `ComponentItem` is set its `category` (which is used as sort of type-checker) and set its `defvalue` (to choose the default type).

The reason behind removing context from `Timer` was to reduce memory (specially for MTD build). Almost all of classes/instance within OT are singletons and we can save RAM by not keeping track of  context pointer for callbacks.  We have few cases where this may not be the case (like `Coap` or `DatasetManager`). Here are some approaches we can consider:

1. Short-term we can use the same solution as in #2128.
2. I think the better (longer-term) approach is to change the class model for  `Coap`. We can define a base class `CoapBase` which practically contains everything in current `Coap` except it does not define the `Timer` member variable, and instead keeps track of a `Timer` reference (or pointer) which its sub-classes should define and pass to base class in its constructor.  We can define different sub-classes of `CoapBase` for different purposes (and ensure each sub-classes is a singleton). 

Thought?

@jwhui I have four devices. One device (call it "server") transmits a beacon to ff03::1 (single CoAP, non-confirmable multicast) every 5 seconds. The other three devices (call them "clients") upon receiving this beacon start sending to the "server" heartbeart messages every 5 seconds (single CoAP, non-confirmable unicast). In the test, the devices are at max transmission power and within 5m of each other.

I finally got the system to run for 36h without any issues, with

- Using latest code from from #2401
- 115200 baud rate
- hardware flow control
- all NCP logging disabled
- OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_NCP_TX_BUFFER_SIZE increased to 4096
- OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_NCP_UART_TX_CHUNK_SIZE increased to 512
- uart.c modified to use a 256 byte ring buffer being serviced by two UARTDRV_Receive() calls in 32 byte chunks

It's difficult to tell at this point which of these were necessary (will not have time to investigate for a while) but if it was just a matter of having 115200 baud rate, flow control and no logging then once #2401 is merged - this should be solved.

It turns out that my particular problem is caused by an orphaned subprocess which has inherited the HTTPServer's listen file descriptor.  Calling subprocess.Popen with close_fds=True avoids my problem.

https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0446/#id11 indicates that Python 3.2 changed the default
close_fds value from False to True. Otherwise any child processes that linger, even briefly (e.g 5 to 10 sec), can interfere with a restart.

I'm attempting to reproduce the problem as reported originally in a process without forks.

-tim

On 31 July 2017 at 18:37, Bruno Oliveira <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> Before release, how about we create a separate option --group-by, which
> is used when --dist is load? By default, group-by would be none which
> sends tests individually to workers, and group-by=module provides the
> functionality of this PR. Having a separate option would allow us to extend
> the option to support grouping by classes in the future.
>
> So this:
>
> pytest --dist=loadsuite ...
>
> Becomes:
>
> pytest --dist=load --group-by=module ...
>
> What do you guys think?
>
 I'm not very keen on this.  It makes more assumptions that the next
scheduler feature will want different kind of grouping.  If we do this as
just a new scheduler name, e.g. `--dist=loadbunch` or so, then we don't
introduce anything new to any other part of xdist.  If we later add another
grouping and it still makes sense to add `--group-by` then we can add it at
that point.

Anyway, happy to be outvoted though ;-)

To be more precise, our C++ kernel stopped working with TF r1.4's C++ codes. We fixed it by re-compiling everything with TF r1.4 branch, and then added the "==" requirement to make sure the package is always working. The current package may not work with TF r1.5, and once TF r1.5 is released, we'll upgrade our pip package that can work with TF r1.5.

Here's the workaround: (from https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/7856)

>Unfortunately TensorBoard uses a colon as the separator between the optional "run name" and the path in the --logdir flag. That means that --logdir=E:\tmp\tensorflow\mnist\logs is interpreted as a run named E with path \tmp\tensorflow\mnist\logs, which explains why it's sensitive to the current working directory.
>As a quick workaround, you could avoid this problem by always specifying an explicit run name as part of the --logdir flag, e.g.:

>`tensorboard --logdir=training:E:\tmp\tensorflow\mnist\logs`

In the meantime, I was able to use the pretty awesome https://icomoon.io/app/ to create a FontAwesome TTF file with ligature support that works like Symbolset.

I just selected all of the FA icons (available as a preset library in the app) and ran this JS in the Inspector to apply the names as ligatures:

```
inputs = document.querySelectorAll('input[placeholder="ligature1, ligature2"]')
changeEvent = new Event('change')
for(var i = 0; i < inputs.length; i++) {
  name = inputs[i].closest('.glyph').querySelector('.glyphName').innerHTML
  inputs[i].value = name
  inputs[i].dispatchEvent(changeEvent)
}
```

Using the generated TTF (included in the attached ZIP) I can type `home` or `file-text-o` and the words get switched out for the associated icon. It makes designing with the font in Photoshop much easier than copy-pasting from the cheatsheet.

@davegandy Icomoon also generated CSS and web-fonts with the ligatures applied (included in the ZIP). I haven't tried out any of the files except the TTF font (as Photoshop was the use case I was interested in) but it might be worth seeing how @Keyamoon has handle ligature support.

I don't think we should add `_cmdloop()` to the end of `do_load`.  `_cmdloop` sets readline configuration for the duration of the loop and then restores the original config when finished. `_cmdloop` stores those settings in member variables on `self`, and not in any kind of stack, so recursive calls are a no-no. If there was already an existing `_cmdloop` running, which executed the `do_load`, then the nested call to `_cmdloop` would stomp on the config and readline settings wouldn't be restored correctly. `_cmdloop` also cleans up the command queue and current script directory stack, so there will be similar issues with those.

We could make `do_load` aware of whether there is a running `_cmdloop` already and then only start `_cmdloop` if there is not. However, that doesn't cover the use case of a 3rd party command loop. In that case `do_load` would see there is no `_cmdloop` and start it running to drain the `cmdqueue`. After this, it will attempt to read more commands from stdin and, undoubtedly, interfere with or block whatever the 3rd party command loop was trying to do. This could be solved by requiring that the 3rd party loop set some sort of flag to make `do_load` aware that there is a loop already processing the `cmdqueue`, but I want to avoid introducing yet more states and flags and modes into the logic. I think that will complexify the implementation and unnecessarily couple things together. More succinctly: maintenance hazard.

Here is my proposal that looks to me like it will work for your use case:

1. Provide a convenience method that does the right thing for you.
    * Maybe called `runcmds_plus_hooks`.
    * Takes an iterable of command strings to run. It will call `onecmd_plus_hooks` on each of them, and also on any commands that get loaded into `cmdqueue`.
2. Document that `onecmd_plus_hooks` requires the `cmdqueue` to be drained. 
    * Warn that the executed command may place commands in the `cmdqueue`. Currently only "load" does this, but maybe that could change in the future...
    * The caller must check the queue and pop commands off to give them to subsequent invocations of `onecmd_plus_hooks` if there is not already an event loop that is responsible for doing this.

Number 2 will document and preserve the existing behavior for `onecmd_plus_hooks` and allow finer grain control by custom event loops. Number 1 will allow you to do what you need with minimal fuss. All you need to do is create a list of all the operations you want to do (setup, body, cleanup, etc), and pass it to `runcmds_plus_hooks`. Example:
```python
runcmds_plus_hooks(["load foo", "do the tql thing", "load bar"])
```

How does that sound? Any objections?

(I believe `asyncio` uses `inspect` these days, and `inspect` is a much lighter import than `asyncio`.) > The only reason to require exactly Python coroutine functions is when you depend on their C-level details or other implementation specifics

We don't care about the C-level details, but in general I think we assume that coroutine objects really implement the *full* coroutine interface, including the `cr_*` attributes. Technically the `cr_*` attributes are not part of the `abc.Coroutine` interface. Do Cython coroutines aim to expose the full coroutine interface, or just the `abc.Coroutine` interface?

* I'd like to keep the `trio.*` namespace as clean as possible, and `trio.Formatter` is pretty confusing. Really I'd rather not have to expose this at all -- see below -- but if we have to then `trio.LoggingFormatter` would be better.
* I'd really rather not force every Trio user have to paste in a bunch of boilerplate just to get useful logs. It would be much nicer if we could autoconfigure this for people -- it's not like anyone *wants* partial tracebacks, or that there are lots of different ways to do basic traceback formatting.

...so putting those together, I guess what I'm saying is... what do you think about monkeypatching `logging.Formatter.formatException` at import time?

I'm cringing just writing that, but we've kind of lost our innocence here already – we customize `sys.excepthook` at import time, and that interface really is just "hey we put this here so you can monkeypatch it". It also would have the advantage that it would automatically apply to subclasses who inherit the default `formatException`, so e.g. we don't have to make our own `trio.BufferingFormatter`. And as icky as it sounds, I can't really think of any situations where it would cause a problem -- mostly it would mean that people just get better logs automatically.

> KeyboardInterrupt is only raised on the outer Nursery (at which point the nested nurseries, tasks, and incoming sockets have been dismantled)

This is partly a matter of luck – `KeyboardInterrupt` can get raised absolutely anywhere. But it does tend to end up in the outermost task because that's who gets it if the `KeyboardInterrupt` arrives at a moment when no tasks are running. You can think of this as, it's getting raised right at the end of the outermost nursery block, and then triggering the regular nursery cleanup logic. Which, of course, starts by cancelling that outermost nursery's cancel scope.

> KeyboardInterrupt triggers a trio.Cancelled in tasks (which can be caught) but not in children nursery scopes. If we push enough state down into the tasks this might be workable, but it would be easier to handle at the parent/nursery level.

What do you mean that children nursery scopes don't get a Cancelled exception? Once the outermost nursery has its cancel scope cancelled, then *everything* (children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren, ...) below it should get a `Cancelled` exception the next time it executes a checkpoint.

Oh, actually, I have a guess – there are several grandchildren that get Cancelled exceptions, and those propagate into their parent, one of the first-level children. And since it's supervising multiple grandchildren and they all crashed, it bundles their exceptions up into a `MultiError`. So if you do `except Cancelled:`, that won't catch a `MultiError` representing multiple `Cancelled`s. A simple check would be to catch `BaseException as exc` and `print(repr(exc))`. Unfortunately `MultiErrors` are not *super* easy to work with because they don't fit very neatly into Python's idea of how exceptions work, but there are some tools to help, in particular `with MultiError.catch(filter_callback): ...`, where `filter_callback` gets called with all the individual exceptions and can let them continue to propagate, catch them, or replace them with a different exception.

> `finally` seems the only way to trigger code as we leave a child nursery but as soon as we await, we exit (presumably because the scope has been cancelled)

As noted above, catching `MultiError` will also work, but if you want to run something every time you exit then `finally` is a better choice, or if you want to run something on all exceptional exits then an `except: ... raise` block is totally legit. But yes, once a scope has been cancelled, all blocking operations are disabled. [See this section](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-core.html#cancellation-semantics), in particular the discussion of "level triggered" cancellation.

> What is the best way to trigger "awaitable" code (eg send one last message) as we exit a child nursery from a KeyboardInterrupt?

So the fundamental challenge here is that there isn't really any way to know "why" you were cancelled – maybe the peer has totally disappear and a timeout finally expired, maybe there was a KeyboardInterrupt and the program is shutting down, ... and this is intentional, because it turns out that once you allow multiple "types" of cancellation it makes life really messy really fast. What if a cancel scope had its timeout expire *and* someone called `.cancel()` on it explicitly, how should that be signaled, that kind of thing.

One option is discussed in that doc section I linked above: while a scope that's been cancelled prevents all blocking operations inside it by default, you *can* disable this by wrapping your "say goodbye" code in a new cancel scope with its `shield` attribute set to `True`. I strongly recommend that if you do this, you also set a sensible timeout on the shield cancel scope, because if the remote host doesn't respond or whatever then your goodbye code will get forever and there's nothing anything outside the shield can do to stop it. But for your use case a timeout of like 0.1 seconds might be plenty, if it's just a best effort attempt to stick a few bytes on the wire before exiting. Of course, this will then run no matter why your code was cancelled.

Alternatively, if you really want control-C specifically to be treated differently than other kinds of shutdown/crash/cancellation events, you absolutely can set up a custom handler for it that does whatever clever thing you want. For example, you could set a global flag `exiting_due_to_KeyboardInterrupt = True` and then raise KeyboardInterrupt, and then your individual tasks could consult that flag. There's an example [here](https://vorpus.org/blog/control-c-handling-in-python-and-trio/#what-if-you-want-a-manual-control-c-handler). The downside is that this kind of custom handler doesn't work if your code gets stuck in an infinite loop and isn't letting trio schedule tasks, but life always has trade-offs... I guess it would also be possible to make a custom signal handler that sets that flag and then invokes trio's normal control-C logic. There's some discussion of this in #134, but if you want to hack it you could do something like:
```python
trio_SIGINT_handler = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGINT)
def my_SIGINT_handler(signo, frame):
    # set our flag:
    global got_KeyboardInterrupt
    got_KeyboardInterrupt = True
    # then run trio's normal handler:
    trio_SIGINT_handler(signo, frame)
signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, my_SIGINT_handler)
```

Does any of that help? It's kind of hard to give specific advice without knowing what you're ultimately trying to do :-)

> Can we have a "task that listens for control-C"? I thought this was usually caught by the outer nursery itself after all tasks had canceled.

By default, `KeyboardInterrupt` gets delivered to some totally arbitrary place. In practice, it will *often* end up being delivered to the main task, which is sitting in the nursery `__aexit__`, and gets handled like any exception that happens inside the nursery scope (i.e., triggers a cancellation of everything in that nursery, and then gets propagated). The reason this is common is that it's what trio does if the control-C happens at a moment when the program is idle (no tasks currently running). But there's no guarantee of this – by default, it could be delivered into the middle of some delicate connection management code or whatever.

[This blog post](https://vorpus.org/blog/control-c-handling-in-python-and-trio/) goes into a lot more detail about the trade-offs of different ways of handling control-C. In particular, there's [discussion](https://vorpus.org/blog/control-c-handling-in-python-and-trio/#option-1-keyboardinterrupt) of why the default `KeyboardInterrupt` behavior (in both regular Python and in trio) is pretty much incompatible with guaranteeing a correct graceful cleanup, and also [an example of how to write task that listens for control-C](https://vorpus.org/blog/control-c-handling-in-python-and-trio/#what-if-you-want-a-manual-control-c-handler). In the example it just does `raise KeyboardInterrupt`, but it could just as easily do an `await program_state.take_snapshot()` first and then cancel everything, or any other arbitrary thing you want.

> the four shielding state approach sounds the most elegant although I realize this is beginning to also threaten the elegant simplicity you currently have :-)

Yeah, I'm not sure... some kind of graceful shutdown is a pretty common/fundamental desire, so maybe it's worth it? But this is an area where trio's already ahead of the competition, and it's extremely tricky, and novel, so I'll probably let it stew for a while before making any decisions.

One thing that did occur to me is that I've already been thinking that in code like `SSLStream` that's "fragile" against cancellation (i.e., it can cause the object's internal state to become corrupted and unusable), it might be a good idea to have some guard that force-closes the stream on cancellation, like:
```python
async def recv(self, max_bytes):
    try:
        # ... complicated stuff here ...
    except Cancelled:
        # our state is broken, but at least we can make sure that any attempt to use it fails fast
        self.forceful_close()
        raise
```

If I wrapped that up into a context manager like `with cleanup_on_cancelled(self.forceful_close): ...`, then that context manager could *also* set the flag that prevents soft cancellations from being delivered inside it if soft-cancellation handling is enabled – since these are two strategies for handling this kind of fragile state, they're needed in the same places.

Hmm.

There is no event loop object in trio, and asyncio has been jumping through hoops to remove explicit loop passing as much as possible, since users dislike it so much. But I guess this doesn't actually matter for your request anyway? It sounds like you don't actually care about the manual passing per se; you'd just as happy with implicit passing, so long as it let you choose which loop was implicitly passed?

The larger problem is that different libraries actually work differently. If trio had an event loop object, its methods would have a totally different API then all the other libraries. In curio, any operation that wakes up a task has to be marked async (e.g. `Lock.release` or `Event.set`); in trio this would be a very weird thing to do. In trio-asyncio, we have to have explicit functions `run_trio`, `run_asyncio` to pass between the two sets of semantics, because the cancellation systems work differently and we need to translate between them. I suspect any API that tries to silently paper over these kinds of differences is going to end up making a lot of compromises and feel weird everywhere; I'd rather trio's builtins focus on taking full advantage of trio's in unique approach.

That said, you certainly can try building this kind of event-loop interface if you want – it doesn't have to be built into trio/curio/whatever to work. (Obviously, since the whole point is that it should work even when it's not native :-).) The `multio` library is one attempt along these lines that I know of. I know @SunDwarf has used and worked on it – they might be able to give you some thoughts on the approach.

I may have a solution for our trouble #27 ;-)

The idea is to consider each fixture as an async context manager, this way setup and teardown steps are both done from the same coroutine.

On top of that, it allow us to catch an exception raised from the user test function, leave the context managers of the fixture like everything is fine, then re-raise the exception.
This way fixtures teardown work just as expected (minus the fact that an exception from the fixture setup/teardown is still seen by pytest as a test FAILURE where it should be an ERROR).

according to RFC4271 (section 6.3), do not set multiple MP-REACH NLRIs in the UPDATE message.
>   If any attribute appears more than once in the UPDATE message, then
>   the Error Subcode MUST be set to Malformed Attribute List.

see also error handling draft (https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-idr-error-handling-19).
>   g.  If the MP_REACH_NLRI attribute or the MP_UNREACH_NLRI [RFC4760]
>       attribute appears more than once in the UPDATE message, then a
>       NOTIFICATION message MUST be sent with the Error Subcode
>       "Malformed Attribute List".  If any other attribute (whether
>       recognized or unrecognized) appears more than once in an UPDATE
>       message, then all the occurrences of the attribute other than the
>       first one SHALL be discarded and the UPDATE message continue to
>       be processed.

Margins help, especially for newcomers. I personally like margins that can be tweaked by the user. Something like a setting

Margin 1: 7
Margin 2: 12
Margin 3: 73
Margin 4: 80

If set to  "0" the margin is disabled (not shown).
Different dialects use other margins than the standard ones (you can setup GnuCOBOL in this way, too) where "hard" margins don't help (and the margin for column 12 wouldn't be necessary for COBOL2002+ as you can code in column 8 and start sections in column 12 [the way GnuCOBOL handles this, too - nonetheless I'd recommend to "code like COBOL85" concerning the margins]). This is the reason why I won't use the caption "Area A, B, ...".

Despite of the optical adjustment this could have an edit purpose - and be useful for "oldtimers", too.
On [CTRL]+[TAB] you could position to the next margin set, with [SHIFT]+[CTRL]+[TAB] position to the previous margin (ending in column 1).

> We cannot guarantee that FS.getsize will only return int, as the size attribute may not be in the details namespace even if that namespace is present. So I made the FS.getsize return Optional[int], but I'll add overrides so that most of its subclasses (OSFS, MemoryFS, etc.) return always int.

The [info docs](https://docs.pyfilesystem.org/en/latest/info.html#details-namespace) mandate that a `size` key will always be available on the `details` namespace, so I think its fare to say that `FS.getsize` will always return an int. In other words; dependencies will be either soft dependencies or hard dependencies.

- Hard dependencies will require a specific version installed (this should in a perfect world only consist of python-only packages)
- Soft dependencies will only require the package (and should not force upgrades)

E.g.
- six is a hard dependency (we need above 1.3)
- numpy is a soft dependency

Might add; there is nothing wrong with having (sane) hard dependencies in requirements.txt for developers. A developer should definitely have reasonable updated numpy/scipy/etc.

mitya57 is right on both points.

We definitely don't want to change the English stemmer in this way, though adding the modified stemmer as a variant would be reasonable I think (c.f. german and german2).

It's also unhelpful to have mixed 3 essentially unrelated changes in one PR.

FWIW, I'm not terribly enthusiastic about adding multiple build systems.  I've seen this tried on other projects, and it doesn't play out well.  Unless someone is really committed to keeping the extra ones up to date, all but the main one will inevitably bitrot.  Or else you require contributors to update all of them and add an extra obstacle to contributing (especially true when one build system can only be tested with proprietary tools).  The last time this came up, I suggested the additional build system be maintained in a forked repo, which allows its maintainer to merge in changes and update the build system for them on his or her own schedule.

I don't have access to a network printer so this is not tested, at least by me ;)

The `escpos.printer.Network` constructor has a `timeout` param that defaults to 60 seconds.
You can pass a different value and wait for the timeout to expire, then an exception must be raised by the `socket` library.

So please try:
```
from escpos import printer as escpos_printer
from escpos.escpos import EscposIO
import time

with EscposIO(escpos_printer.Network(192.168.1.1, port=9100, timeout=10), autocut=True) as printer_socket:
    printer_socket.writelines('Test Line 1')
    time.sleep(5)  # Turn-off the printer and wait for at least 10 secons, an exception must be raised.
    printer_socket.writelines('Test Line 2')
    printer_socket.writelines('Test Line 3')
    printer_socket.writelines('Test Line 4')
    printer_socket.writelines('Test Line 5')
```
Let us know if it worked or no exception is raised at all.

I mentioned this before - I think this needs to really save the state and restore it - as it stands its resetting the fixture, which loses state.

for instance:

```
useFixture(..)
with ignore_deprecations_here:
  warnings.filter.... some thing here
   ...
do_a_thing_that_should_be_filtered()
```

will fail today, I believe - and its not obvious that it will.

More seriously, I'm fairly sure that 

```
useFixture(..)
warnings.filter.... some thing here
with ignore_deprecations_here:
   ...
do_a_thing_that_should_be_filtered()
```

will also fail: - because the cleanup called in ignore_deprecations_here() will wipe it out: and this is even less obvious.

So - what I'm proposing is that ignore_deprecations_here should not exit-and-enter: it should pop and insert the single filter that matters.

@lukasniemeier-zalando @i8r copying my comment from #268:

I think this behavior is due to how CloudFormation works:

* Senza `create` creates a new CloudFormation (CF) stack with VersionDomain and MainDomain DNS records
  * MainDomain has weight zero for this stack version and is a "managed by CF" resource
* Senza `traffic` will change the weight of the MainDomain record without changing the CF template
  * MainDomain is therefore no longer exactly the same resource as originally created by CF
* Senza `delete` will delete the CF stack
  * MainDomain will not be deleted by CF as the resource no longer matches the original "CF managed" resource

So basically we are mixing CloudFormation and manual changes (directly using Route 53 API), i.e. the manually changed MainDomain DNS record leaves the scope of CF management during the lifetime.

You can observe that a brand new stack will be completely deleted by CF if you don't use `senza traffic` during its lifetime.

After getting a better understanding of your intent with leadership (and config) states, I finally see where you are going with this.  As far as naming, our current phases are "hook" and "other"; I think "other" should probably be changed to "states" or "main".  For the new states / decorators, I think I'm partial to `@setup` and `@teardown`.

> I actually wonder if it might be better to go ahead and add the caching for everything since that would also allow us to 1) avoid calls to the agent which have been a source of extra, unexpected network traffic to the controller, 2) enforce consistency about when changes to charm state are visible (there's currently some ambiguity with, e.g., config data and possibly relation data because the data that's visible can be different prior to the relevant -changed hook triggering, though that's less of an issue when doing reactive "properly").

I don't think `2` is a real issue for us because a reactive charm shouldn't care about which hook something happens in. I think it's actually hard to write a handler that is hook-specific using the latest charms.reactive.

The current behavior also has its advantages, a deployment can settle sooner, because you avoid charms re-configuring the application using outdated info: more hooks will be no-ops.

> If we have a broken_relations collection, it means you'd have to iterate over both endpoint.relations.departed_units and endpoint.broken_relations.departed_units to get all of the departed units, and you wouldn't be able to know ahead of time which collection the currently leaving unit was in. 

I agree, `broken_relations` doesn't seem the right solution for this.


>  But another option would be to have departed units be considered "orphaned" from their relation, and so only live on an Endpoint level collection.

This seems like a good option too, especially because it allows us to do the following:

> We could handle the corner case the same as we now do with departing units: in reactive, a relation is "broken" as soon as its last relation is departing (i.e., its rel.units collection is empty).

> Is it possible to subclass let's say FloatText and add a NumberFormat trait?

Of course! You can use the widget-cookiecutter to get a head start on building a package for it.

But I think even better would be putting in a PR to the FloatText widget to add a `value_format` (or a better name) attribute. The FloatText already understands *input* in scientific notation.

Here is where the widget text gets set for IntText and FloatText: https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/a9c4069570aa92368f590296569e411d9c49e123/packages/controls/src/widget_int.ts#L528

So we could just redirect that to a formatting function, and then implement a formatting function for IntTextView and FloatTextView. You'll notice the check in the code above checks to see if the values are equal to decide whether to update the text, but now we'll actually have to check to see if the formatted strings are equal. This will make the `continuous_update=True` a bit hard to type, but I think that's probably a rare case, and I'm not sure how useful it is.

Full disclosure though - we do have a fluent interface (from traitlets) for the `.tag` function to allow metadata to easily be added in the constructor.

What if we added an on_click argument to the constructor?

This is what I *think* I know how to do (based very much on the way layouts are handled):

+ Add a `MouseListener` widget with options for choosing which events (e.g. click, dblclick, mousemove) that that listener instance will pay attention to.
+ Add a `listener` or `listeners` attribute to `DOMWidgetModel` and the corresponding view, which adds the listener(s) to the widget (e.g. `Image`).

so that something like this is possible:

```python
clicks_only = MouseListener(click=True, dbclick=True, remaining_events=False)
image_widget.listeners = [clicks_only]
```

and then your image widget is clickable.

If I turn that around, to try to make this happen:

```python
clicks_only.source = image_widget
```

then I see two issues:

1. I don't know how make the JS side do anything with MouseListener because there is no view of it. I could force a view by creating the view in the model but that seems....wrong.
2. It seems like the python object ought to know what things it is listening to, so even with this second approach a property will need to be added to the widget (even if it is a hidden property) that keeps track of the listeners.
I see two parts to doing something like this - an autocomplete widget when provides the UI for selecting completions, etc., and also a kernel function that provides the autocompletion data.

Done. Here is a working example:

```python
import datapackage

descriptor = 'http://data.okfn.org/data/core/country-list/datapackage.json'
storage = datapackage.push_datapackage(descriptor, 'pandas')
storage['data___data'].head()
```

You can find full examples here:

https://gist.github.com/sirex/d65113958a062586c1d484921648057d

And the code: https://github.com/sirex/jsontableschema-pandas-py

There is only one thing, that does not work. Currently `datapackage.push_datapackage` does not return storage. Since python Pandas data frames are stored in memory, there is no way how to get them, unless storage is returned by `datapackage.push_datapackage`.

Examples above use slightly modified version of `datapackage-py`, where `storage` instance is returned by `datapackage.push_datapackage`.

Tested this on several datapackages, everything seems to work.

Also there is support for pulling existing Pandas dataframes to a datapackage.

I had some experience in running calculations with EDIFFG (e.g -0.03) using custodian. These calcs start from the MP double-relaxed structures. I still met many errors (~10%). I would say the error rate is much higher than that of MP double relaxation if we do it for a new crystal structure. 

In my experience, the kpts scheme of MP is highly reliable (the kpts density is much high). This is likely to the reason why there is no much difference in the calculated band gap between MP and (MP+ EDIFF=1e-5;EDIFFG=-0.01) for band structure calc of an insulator. However, for magnetic systems, the EDIFFG has a significant impact on the calculated magnetization.

EDIFFG = -0.05 is a good choice, which is widely used in surface calc in order to minimize the computational cost(comparing with EDIFFG=-0.01). In the long run, I agree with SP's recommendation that MP should consider adding the force criterion given that it is of great importance for some properties calc.

The errors appearing in calculations with force criterion are handlable. The problem is that Custodian at the moment misses some error handlers for these calcs. We only need to add new error handlers into Custodian to deal with these situations. So I believe it is feasible to add force criterion in MP.

It is probably best if we try to list all possible options before deciding on one.

Just to continue listing potential options, another one is to change featurize() so that it returns a NamedTuple of (values, labels). We would get rid of the feature_labels() method altogether - featurize() would return both data and labels. One reason to do this is that the initial sketch of the BaseFeaturizer assumed that feature_labels() were an attribute of the featurizer (plus any initialization options). But, after coding many of these featurizers, it really turns out to be an attribute of the featurizer plus the data thrown into it in many cases.

Some something like:

```
values, labels = MyFeaturizer.featurize(data)
```

or a different usage to highlight the NamedTuple aspect:

```
x = MyFeaturizer.featurize(data)
values = x.values
labels = x.labels
```
